Sadly for Cormier, that's the right answer. Was it bad luck for Cormier that such a significant illegal strike, and one that referee Marc Goddard missed completely, held such a key role in DC's eventual loss? Yes. But it's also karma, just the same. Cormier got away with noticeably bad eye pokes in both of the first two fights during key moments that allowed him to have major success. He also poked Miocic once more in Round 1 of their trilogy bout. Cormier's infraction in the rematch was so vicious that Miocic needed surgery and delayed their third bout more than a year as some questioned if he would ever fight again. Cormier simply has no ground to stand on to try and complain or blame the foul for his loss just as he has no one to blame but himself for abandoning his wrestling after the opening round of both fights against Miocic that he lost. Had Cormier never established the eye pokes as a pattern to their rivalry, there may have been more sympathy thrown his way for how much it affected him in this third fight. Jon Jones has a history of failed drug tests throughout his UFC career. Here are some of the key instances: 

January 2015: Tested positive for cocaine metabolites ahead of UFC 182, but was allowed to compete as cocaine is not banned out-of-competition by the World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA).

July 2016: Failed a drug test for two banned substances (clomiphene and letrozole) before UFC 200, leading to a one-year suspension and being pulled from the event. USADA determined he did not knowingly ingest the substances, finding they resulted from a contaminated supplement.

August 2017: Tested positive for the anabolic steroid turinabol after his UFC 214 fight against Daniel Cormier. This resulted in his win being overturned to a no contest, another stripping of his title, and a 15-month suspension. USADA again stated he didn't intentionally cheat but was given the 15-month ban due to "substantial assistance" in other anti-doping cases.

December 2018: A trace amount of turinabol, the same substance found in his 2017 test, was detected in a pre-fight drug test. However, based on revised USADA policy and the low level detected, it was concluded this was a "pulsing effect" from the previous positive test and not a new violation.
